one_line_takeaway: SHARPEN combines Deep SHAP-based coarse-to-fine fault localization with CMA-ES derivative-free optimization to repair backdoored, adversarially-vulnerable, or unfair neural networks, reporting BSR/ASR/unfairness reductions of roughly 95-99.97% with under ~2% average accuracy loss and up to two orders of magnitude faster repair time than search-heavy baselines like CARE, though the authors flag Shapley-estimation cost as a scalability bottleneck for larger models.
key_finding: On the paper's own tables: Backdoor removal (Table 2) -- on VGG13, SHARPEN reduces BSR by an average of 98.73% with average accuracy drop of 0.95%; on ResNet18, SHARPEN reduces BSR by an average of 99.97% while improving clean accuracy by an average of 4.67%; AI-Lancet is cited as degrading accuracy by 71.64% on VGG13-BadNet for comparable repair. Adversarial repair (Table 4): SHARPEN reduces ASR by 95.00% on VGG13 (0.66% accuracy cost) and by 99.80% on ResNet18 (0.37% accuracy cost); comparison baselines IR and APRNN are reported with accuracy drops as large as 14.38% and 33.98% respectively. Fairness repair (Table 5): SHARPEN achieves the best average Score (76.21%), decreasing unfairness by an average of 78.05% with an average accuracy decrease of 1.84%; the next-best baseline IDNN achieves 9.69 points less average unfairness reduction while costing more than double the accuracy loss (3.97% vs. 1.84%), though the paper notes IDNN outperforms SHARPEN specifically on the Census (Gender) attribute. Aggregate margins claimed by the authors: up to 10.56% over INNER (backdoor), 5.78% over CARE (adversarial), 11.82% over IDNN (fairness). Efficiency (Table 3): SHARPEN has the lowest average total repair time (80.1s) among compared methods on backdoor removal, e.g. ~181s total on VGG16-ImageNet10 versus CARE's >39,000s. Ablations: Top-k localization is necessary -- Random/Bottom-k neuron selection yields near-zero or negative Score (Table 6) while Top-k reaches Score > 88% across the three tested settings; swapping CMA-ES for RACOS (Table 7) still achieves strong unfairness reduction (87.40% vs. 78.05%) but at greater accuracy cost (-3.02% vs. -1.84%), supporting the claim that localization quality, not the specific optimizer, is the primary driver of repair effectiveness.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is SHARPEN (SHApley-guided neural RePair via derivative-freE optimizatioN), a two-stage neural network repair framework. Stage 1 is a novel Deep SHAP-based white-box fault localization method that performs coarse-to-fine analysis: it first computes a layer contribution score C_L(l) (mean absolute difference between Deep SHAP attribution maps of a clean vs. faulty model on violating inputs) to rerank layers, then computes a neuron contribution score C_N(j) within the top suspicious layers to select Top-K faulty neurons/filters (mapped to concrete FC weights/biases or conv-filter weights). Stage 2 repairs only the identified neurons using CMA-ES (Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy), a derivative-free optimizer, minimizing a 4-term weighted objective D(delta) = w_pro*L_pro/L_pro_init + w_acc*L_acc + w_act*L_act/L_act_init + w_reg*||delta||^2 (property-violation loss, accuracy-preservation loss, activation-distance loss, and an L2 regularizer on the perturbation). The authors state the design goal is to make repair derivative-free (gradient-independent, hence applicable across architectures/activation functions) while confining the high-dimensional CMA-ES search to a small, interpretability-selected neuron subset instead of the whole network.
threat_model: SHARPEN is a defensive/repair technique, not an attack. It assumes a pre-trained network f that already violates a target property on a known violating input set X_fault, and it has white-box access to model internals (weights, activations) plus a labeled benign set X_clean and a violating/attack set X_v (e.g., triggered images for backdoors, FGSM adversarial examples, or discriminatory samples for fairness). Three concrete defect classes are addressed as instantiations of a Unified Attack Success Rate metric: (1) backdoor attacks (BSR metric) via BadNets, WaNet, and Blend triggers embedded by data poisoning (10% poisoned training data, 10 additional fine-tuning epochs on a 100-epoch base model); (2) adversarial attacks (ASR metric) using FGSM-generated examples with perturbation magnitude epsilon = 0.01 and 0.05; (3) individual/statistical unfairness, defined via KL divergence between prediction distributions across sensitive-attribute groups (gender, age, race), following an independence-based fairness formalization adopted from prior work (IDNN paper). The repair objective is formalized as a dual constraint: V(f') <= tau (property violation below threshold) and accuracy on clean data not dropping by more than epsilon_acc relative to the original model.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitation (Conclusion, Section 6): the computational cost of Shapley/Deep SHAP estimation is a bottleneck, especially anticipated for large-scale foundation models and complex generative settings; future work is proposed toward sparsity-aware sampling and extending to LLMs. Additional caveats present in the paper's own text: some repair settings still exceed a 2% accuracy drop despite the framework's general small-utility-loss claim (Section 4.2.2); IDNN outperforms SHARPEN on one specific attribute (Census-Gender) even though SHARPEN wins on average; hyperparameters (w_pro, w_acc, w_act, w_reg, and per-task sub-weights like w_ca/w_asr) are manually configured per task based on 'the severity of the property violation and desired repair priorities,' i.e., some task-specific tuning is required rather than being fully automatic; adversarial evaluation uses only FGSM (a relatively weak, single-step attack), and no results are reported for VGG16-ImageNet10 under the adversarial task (marked '-' in Table 1), suggesting adversarial evaluation is narrower than backdoor evaluation.

quantitative_results: Backdoor removal VGG13: avg BSR reduction 98.73%, avg accuracy drop 0.95%
Backdoor removal ResNet18: avg BSR reduction 99.97%, avg accuracy improvement 4.67%
Adversarial repair VGG13 (FGSM): ASR reduction 95.00%, accuracy cost 0.66%
Adversarial repair ResNet18 (FGSM): ASR reduction 99.80%, accuracy cost 0.37%
Fairness repair average: unfairness reduction 78.05%, accuracy decrease 1.84%, average Score 76.21%
Repair time (Table 3): SHARPEN avg total 80.1s vs CARE avg 13,655s; on VGG16-ImageNet10 SHARPEN ~181s (12.32s locate + 168.8s repair) vs CARE 34,324s (locate) + 5,543s (repair)
Sensitivity: Score peaks at Top-K=20 selected neurons (95.66-98.19% range across K in {5,10,20,40,80})
Ablation (Table 6): Random/Bottom-k neuron selection gives near-zero or negative Score (e.g., BadNets-VGG13 Random Score -1.02, Bottom-k -0.09) vs SHARPEN Top-k Score 98.52
Optimizer ablation (Table 7): CMA-ES avg unfairness reduction 78.05% (accuracy cost 1.84%) vs RACOS 87.40% (accuracy cost 3.02%)
